1: 'When it was decided that we would sail for Italy, Paul and some other prisoners were handed over to a centurion named Julius, who belonged to the Imperial Regiment' — the 'we' (hēmas — us) resumes the we-narrative that began at Acts 16:10, dropped at Acts 17:1, resumed at Acts 20:5, and continues now through the Rome arrival. Luke's presence in the voyage is a firsthand claim for the narrative's accuracy. The 'Imperial Regiment' (Sebastēs speirēs — Augustan cohort) is an elite designation; Julius's rank and regiment explain the preferential treatment Paul receives (Acts 27:3). Acts 27 is the most detailed sea-voyage narrative in ancient literature, with precise nautical terminology throughout.
2: 'We boarded a ship from Adramyttium about to sail for ports along the coasts of the province of Asia, and we put out to sea. Aristarchus, a Macedonian from Thessalonica, was with us' — the Adramyttium ship (Adramyttēnō ploiō — a ship belonging to Adramyttium, a port on the Asia Minor coast) is a coastal vessel making local runs, not a direct Italy-bound ship; Julius must find a connecting vessel. Aristarchus (Acts 19:29; Acts 20:4; Colossians 4:10; Phlm 24) is Paul's Thessalonian companion who was seized in the Ephesian riot — his presence on the prison ship suggests he traveled as Paul's 'slave' (a common legal fiction allowing companions to accompany prisoners).
3: 'The next day we landed at Sidon; and Julius, in kindness to Paul (philanthrōpōs — philanthropically/humanely), allowed him to go to his friends so they might provide for his needs' — philanthrōpōs (with human-kindness, humanely) is the word for the Roman humanitarian ideal: Julius treats Paul as a person of dignity rather than merely a prisoner. 'To his friends' (pros tous philous — to the friends/beloved) — the Sidon Christian community. The pattern of Paul's personal liberty within custody (receiving visitors, writing letters, preaching) continues throughout his imprisonment in Acts (Acts 23:16; Acts 24:23; Acts 27:3; Acts 28:16, 30).
4: 'From there we put out to sea again and passed to the lee of Cyprus because the winds were against us' — the route from Sidon to Myra (Acts 27:5) requires sailing north of Cyprus to gain shelter from the prevailing NW Etesian winds. The nautical detail ('passed to the lee' — hypepleusamēn — sailed under the wind-shadow of Cyprus) is exactly the seamanship a knowledgeable observer would record. Luke's narrative is not a theological allegory but a maritime diary with theological significance — the voyage's dangers are real dangers that real seamanship addressed.
5: 'When we had sailed across the open sea off the coast of Cilicia and Pamphylia, we landed at Myra in Lycia' — the open-sea passage (pelagos — deep open sea, not coastal waters) off Cilicia and Pamphylia covers the southern Turkish coast, where the sea is exposed without shelter. Myra (modern Demre) in Lycia was a major port where Alexandrian grain ships stopped on their way to Italy — the centurion's transfer to an Alexandrian ship (Acts 27:6) is not coincidental but reflects the standard route of the Alexandrian grain fleet.
6: 'There the centurion found an Alexandrian ship sailing for Italy and put us on board' — the Alexandrian grain fleet was the lifeline of Rome: Egypt supplied a large portion of Rome's grain, and the great cargo ships (navis oneraria — merchant/cargo ships) that carried it were among the largest vessels of antiquity. Being placed on such a vessel (276 people in Acts 27:37 is consistent with large grain-ship capacity) means traveling on a ship whose commercial importance gave it relative priority in Mediterranean transit. The centurion's authority allows him to commandeer passage on the grain ship.
7: 'We made slow headway for many days and had difficulty arriving off Cnidus. When the wind did not allow us to hold our course, we sailed to the lee of Crete, opposite Salmone' — the slow progress (bradyploeountes — slow-sailing, a rare compound) and difficulty (molis — with difficulty, barely) reflects the NW headwinds that Alexandrian ships routinely struggled against in the late summer. Cnidus is at the SW tip of Asia Minor; from there the ship should have turned north into the Aegean but the winds forced it south under Crete. Salmone is Crete's NE cape.
8: 'We moved along the coast with difficulty and came to a place called Fair Havens, near the town of Lasea' — Fair Havens (Kaloi Limenes — Beautiful Harbors) is a real anchorage on Crete's south coast, still known by this name. The detailed place-names (Lasea, Fair Havens, later Phoenix — Acts 27:12) demonstrate that Luke is not composing a generic sea-voyage but recording a specific itinerary that can be followed on a map. The mention of Lasea as a nearby town anchors the account geographically.
9: 'Much time had been lost, and sailing had already become dangerous because by now it was after the Day of Atonement (tēn nēsteian — the Fast). So Paul warned them' — the Fast (hē nēsteia — the fasting; Yom Kippur) falls in early October. Ancient navigation treatises identified the period from mid-September to early November as progressively dangerous and after mid-November as closed. The Jewish liturgical calendar thus provides the chronological marker for the dangerous season: the Day of Atonement has passed, and the Mediterranean is turning hostile. Paul's knowledge of navigation seasons adds another dimension to his authority in the crisis ahead.
10: 'Men, I can see that our voyage is going to be disastrous and bring great loss to ship and cargo, and to our own lives also' — Paul's prophetic warning (theōrō hoti — I perceive/see that) uses the vision-verb but functions here as practical discernment based on experience and divine awareness rather than a formal vision. The warning is not yet based on divine revelation (that comes in Acts 27:23-24) but on practical assessment of conditions. Paul the experienced traveler (2 Corinthians 11:25 — three times shipwrecked) has navigational wisdom alongside prophetic authority.
11: 'But the centurion, instead of listening to what Paul said, followed the advice of the pilot and of the owner of the ship' — the centurion's preference for professional navigators over the prisoner's counsel is entirely natural and legally appropriate. The nautical experts outrank Paul in maritime matters. Luke records the decision without editorializing; the narrative's subsequent development (the storm, Paul's angel-vision, his renewed authority) is the commentary on who should have been heeded.
12: 'Since the harbour was unsuitable to winter in, the majority decided that we should sail on, hoping to reach Phoenix and winter there' — Phoenix (Phoinika — modern Loutra) was a harbor on the SW coast of Crete that faced SW and NW — offering protection from the dangerous winter storms from NE. The decision is a calculated risk: leave the inadequate anchorage at Fair Havens for the better harbor 50 miles west. 'The majority' (hoi pleiones — the greater number) — a formal decision by vote of the significant parties: pilot, owner, centurion, and perhaps others.
13: 'When a gentle south wind began to blow, they saw their opportunity; so they weighed anchor and sailed along the shore of Crete' — the southerly (notos — south wind) gave the ship a favorable push westward along Crete's south coast. 'Thinking that they had obtained what they desired' (doxantes tēs protheseōs kekratēkenai — thinking they had mastered their purpose) — the sailors' confidence that conditions have aligned with their plans is immediately refuted. The southerly is a brief window before the violent NE wind that will follow; favorable conditions are not divine approval.
14: 'Before very long, a wind of hurricane force, called the Northeaster (Eurakylōn), swept down from the island' — the Eurakylōn (a compound of the Greek euros + Latin aquilo = east wind + north wind) is the Levantine name for the violent NE gale that blows off the Cretan mountains in winter. Luke's use of the sailors' technical name for the wind is a mark of authenticity: he records the mariners' vocabulary, not a generalized 'storm.' The wind's origin 'from the island' (kat' autēs — down from it, i.e., from Crete's mountains) explains its katabatic (descending) force.
15: 'The ship was caught by the storm and could not head into the wind; so we gave way to it and were driven along' — the nautical phrase 'could not head into the wind' (mē dynamenēs antophthalmein tō anemō — not able to look the wind in the face) is the seamanship term for a ship unable to beat upwind. 'We gave way' (epidontes — handing over, yielding) — the crew abandoned the attempt to maintain course and let the wind drive them. The ship is now entirely at the storm's mercy; human navigation has yielded to the elements.
16: 'As we passed to the lee of a small island called Cauda, we were hardly able to make the lifeboat secure' — Cauda (modern Gavdos, the southernmost Greek island, 23 miles SW of Crete) provided brief shelter for emergency repairs. 'Hardly able' (molis — with difficulty, barely) appears twice in the chapter (Acts 27:7, 16) emphasizing the sustained difficulty. The skaphē (lifeboat) was normally towed behind the ship; in heavy seas it would waterlog and sink, so bringing it aboard was urgent but extremely difficult work.
17: 'So the men hoisted it aboard. Then they passed ropes under the ship itself to hold it together. Fearing that they would run aground on the sandbars of Syrtis, they lowered the sea anchor and let the ship be driven along' — 'passed ropes under the ship' (hypozōnnyntes to ploion — undergirding the ship) is frapping: passing cables around the ship's hull to prevent the planks from working apart in heavy seas. The Syrtis (Syrtin — the Syrtis Major, shallow sandbar-filled waters off Libya's coast) was one of the ancient world's most feared navigational hazards. Lowering the sea anchor (skeuos — the gear, here likely a drag-anchor or bare spar) slowed the ship's drift.
18: 'We took such a violent battering from the storm that the next day they began to throw the cargo overboard' — ekbolēn epoiounto (they were making a throwing-out) — the jettisoning of cargo (ekbolē = throwing-out, a nautical technical term) is the standard emergency measure in Mediterranean storms. Losing the cargo was financial catastrophe for the ship's owner; it was the only way to lighten the ship and keep it afloat. The sequence of emergency measures — frapping (Acts 27:17), jettisoning cargo (Acts 27:18), jettisoning the ship's tackle (Acts 27:19) — shows the crew executing a rational response to a worsening situation.
19: 'On the third day, they threw the ship's tackle overboard with their own hands' — 'the ship's tackle' (skeuēn tou ploiou — the equipment of the ship) includes the spare yards, sails, ropes, and heavy equipment. 'With their own hands' (autokheiroi — with their own hands, personally) emphasizes that by the third day even the hands-off passengers (like Luke) were throwing equipment overboard. The desperation has passed beyond crew work to involve everyone aboard.
20: 'When neither sun nor stars appeared for many days and the storm continued raging, we finally gave up all hope (elpis — hope; disp=2) of being saved' — elpis (hope/confident expectation; disp=2) is here the existential confidence of survival: the crew has lost the practical confidence that anyone will survive. In ancient navigation, the sun by day and stars by night provided the only positional reference; multiple days of overcast deprived the crew of any knowledge of where they were or where they were heading. Paul's subsequent intervention will restore — through divine assurance rather than navigation — the hope the storm took away.
21: 'After the men had gone a long time without food, Paul stood up before them and said: Men, you should have taken my advice not to sail from Crete; then you would have spared yourselves this damage and loss' — the men's fasting (asitias — foodlessness, abstaining from food) is partly imposed by the conditions (preparing food in a storm-tossed ship is nearly impossible) and partly voluntary (seasickness, despair). Paul's retrospective ('you should have listened to me') is not triumphalism but the establishment of his prophetic credibility: the one whose first warning proved correct is about to deliver a second word that must be heeded.
22: 'But now I urge you to keep up your courage, because not one of you will be lost; only the ship will be destroyed' — the paradox of Paul's second prophecy: physical destruction (the ship) paired with total salvation (not one person will be lost). This distinction between property-loss and life-preservation is the specific content of the divine promise Paul is about to report. The command 'keep up your courage' (euthumeite — be of good cheer, take heart) is the word the Lord used to Paul in the Jerusalem temple vision (Acts 23:11 — 'take courage').
23: 'Last night an angel of the God to whom I belong and whom I serve (paristēmi — who I stand beside/serve) stood beside me' — the angel's appearance in the night parallels Cornelius's angel (Acts 10:3-7) and the Lord's appearance to Paul at Corinth (Acts 18:9) and Jerusalem (Acts 23:11). 'The God to whom I belong' (theō hou eimi — the God whose I am) — the possessive 'whose I am' is the covenant-belonging formula: Paul identifies himself as God's property, not merely God's servant. 'Whom I serve' (latreuō — whose cult/worship I perform) — the priestly-service term for devoted worship.
24: 'And said: Do not be afraid, Paul. You must stand trial before Caesar (dei se Kaisari parastēnai — it is necessary for you to stand before Caesar); and God has graciously given you the lives of all who sail with you' — dei (it is necessary) — the divine necessity-formula used throughout Luke-Acts for the events that must occur to fulfill God's redemptive plan (Luke 2:49; Acts 4:43; Acts 9:22; Acts 24:7; Acts 9:16; Acts 14:22; Acts 19:21; Acts 27:24). Paul must reach Rome because God has determined it; the storm cannot defeat what divine necessity requires. 'Graciously given' (kechāristai — has gifted as a charis-gift, perfect tense = completed and permanent) — 276 lives are Paul's gift from God to distribute; his presence on the ship is a saving mercy for everyone aboard.
25: 'So keep up your courage, men, for I have faith in God (pisteuō tō theō — I believe God; disp=2 for theos) that it will happen just as he told me' — 'I believe God' — not 'I believe in God' (pisteuō eis theon = faith directed toward God as object) but pisteuō tō theō (dative = I trust God's word, I credit his promise). The MKT 'I have faith in God' renders the dative correctly; Paul's confidence is trust in the specific divine word just received, not generalized piety. 'Just as he told me' — the precision of the divine word (ship destroyed, all lives saved) is what Paul trusts; he does not trust a general providence but a specific promise.
26: 'Nevertheless, we must run aground on some island' — the 'nevertheless' (eis nēson dé tina dei hēmas ekpesein — but it is necessary for us to fall on some island) completes the divine word: the ship will be lost but not the people, and the mechanism will be a grounding on an island (fulfilled at Acts 27:41-44, Malta). The dei (necessity) again — even the shipwreck is a divinely ordained event within God's preservation of Paul and his companions. Luke records Paul's prophecy before the event so the reader can see the fulfillment; this is Luke's authentication technique throughout Acts.
27: 'On the fourteenth night we were still being driven across the Adriatic Sea, when about midnight the sailors sensed they were approaching land' — the 'Adriatic Sea' (kata meson tou Adriou — in the middle of the Adriatic) in ancient usage extended south to include the sea between Italy and Greece, the Ionian Sea, and the waters south of Crete — not merely the modern Adriatic between Italy and Yugoslavia. Fourteen nights of storm-driving at the typical drift-rate would bring the ship from Cauda (near Crete) to the vicinity of Malta — the geography is consistent. The sailors' sensory detection ('sensed') probably means they heard breakers in the darkness.
28: 'They took soundings and found that the water was twenty metres deep. A short time later they took soundings again and found it was fifteen metres deep' — the depth soundings (bolisantes — dropping the lead-line) are recorded with nautical precision: 'twenty fathoms' (orguias eikosi — twenty arm-spans, approximately 36 meters) then 'fifteen fathoms' (pente kai deka — fifteen arm-spans, approximately 27 meters). The shallowing water confirms they are approaching a shore. The 'short time later' (brachy diastēsantes — after a small interval/distance) and the rapid decrease in depth suggests a steeply shelving approach to Malta's coast.
29: 'Fearing that we would be dashed against the rocks, they dropped four anchors from the stern and prayed for daylight' — ancient ships could anchor from either end; stern-anchoring keeps the bow (and thus the steering oars) pointing toward shore, enabling a controlled beaching attempt at first light. 'Four anchors' distributed the holding force and reduced the risk of any single anchor failing. 'Prayed for daylight' (ēuchonto hēmeran genesthai — they were wishing/praying for day to come) — even the pagan sailors pray; Paul's God is not the only one addressed during the crisis, though he is the only one who answers.
30: 'In an attempt to escape from the ship, the sailors let the lifeboat down into the sea, pretending they were going to lower some anchors from the bow' — the sailors' plan to abandon ship is a reasonable survival strategy from their perspective: escape in the lifeboat before the ship breaks up. 'Pretending' (prophasis — pretext/false reason) they were laying bow anchors gave them cover for lowering the lifeboat. The sailors know the bow-anchor story is false; the soldiers and passengers may not have known the difference. Paul sees through the ruse (or receives divine insight) and intervenes.
31: 'Then Paul said to the centurion and the soldiers: Unless these men stay with the ship, you cannot be saved' — Paul's warning to Julius uses a conditional that is also a divine-word transmission: the angel promised 276 people would be saved, but that promise requires everyone to remain aboard. The sailors' professional competence is needed for the controlled beaching attempt at dawn; their desertion would doom the remaining passengers. The divine promise is not unconditional in execution: it requires the human cooperation it prescribes.
32: 'So the soldiers cut the ropes that held the lifeboat and let it drift away' — the soldiers' decisive action (apekopsan — they cut off, aorist = decisive, immediate action) eliminates the escape option for everyone, including themselves. The irrevocability of cutting the lifeboat loose commits all 276 people to the ship's fate — exactly what the divine word required. Julius's decision to trust Paul's word over the sailors' professional judgment is the centurion's act of faith: he has watched Paul's prophecy prove accurate for two weeks.
33: 'Just before dawn Paul urged them all to eat. For the last fourteen days, he said, you have been in constant suspense and have gone without food — you have not eaten anything' — 'constant suspense' (prosdokōntes — waiting/expecting anxiously, present participle = ongoing anxious watching) has kept the crew in hypervigilant watchfulness for fourteen days. 'Gone without food' (asitoi diateleite — you have continued foodless) — the asitia (foodlessness) of Acts 27:21 confirmed. Paul's initiative in calling the community to eat before the final beaching attempt is a pastoral act: he recognizes that frightened, exhausted, starved people cannot execute the physical demands of survival.
34: 'Now I urge you to take some food. You need it to survive. Not one of you will lose a single hair from his head (thriks apo tēs kephalēs oudenos apoleitai — not a hair from the head of anyone will be lost)' — the 'not a hair' formula is the OT idiom for absolute divine protection (1 Samuel 14:45; 2 Samuel 14:11; 1 Kings 1:52; Luke 21:18). Paul's guarantee of physical preservation uses covenant-protection language: God's word of preservation is as certain as his word of judgment. The contrast: the ship will be destroyed (Acts 27:22) but not a hair on any person will be lost — two specific, contrasting divine guarantees.
35: 'After he said this, he took some bread and gave thanks to God (eucharistēsen tō theō — gave eucharist/thanks to God; disp=2 for theos) in front of them all. Then he broke it and began to eat' — the Eucharistic sequence (took bread, gave thanks, broke it, ate) is identical to the sequence of the Last Supper (Luke 22:19 — took, gave thanks, broke, gave) and the Emmaus meal (Luke 24:30 — took, blessed, broke, gave). Luke does not call this a communion meal — Paul is not offering the Lord's Supper to 276 people including pagan sailors and soldiers — but he narrates it with the full eucharistic gesture-sequence, allowing the reader to see that Paul's table-fellowship amid catastrophe bears the marks of the Lord's table.
36: 'They were all encouraged and ate some food themselves' — euthumoi genomoi (becoming cheerful/of good cheer) uses the same word as Paul's 'keep up your courage' (euthumeite) in Acts 27:22 and Acts 27:25. The food and Paul's assurance have done what fourteen days of storm could not: restored the crew's euthumia. The eating 'themselves' (kai autoi — even they) suggests that the pagan crew participated in the meal that Paul's prayer inaugurated — an unexpected table-fellowship across the gulf of religion, ethnicity, and social status.
37: 'Altogether there were 276 of us on board' — the number 276 (psychai diakosiai hebdomēkonta heks — 276 souls/lives) is a specific count that functions as a dramatic countdown to the promised preservation: 276 people will be saved, every single one. 'Souls' (psychai — souls, lives) is the OT idiom for persons (Genesis 46:27 — 'seventy souls'). The number is also a check on the promise: if even one person fails to reach shore, the prophecy will have failed. Luke records the count precisely so the reader can verify that the divine word's 'not one will be lost' was exactly fulfilled.
38: 'When they had eaten as much as they wanted, they lightened the ship by throwing the grain into the sea' — the final jettisoning of the grain cargo (sitou ektyphloumenoi — throwing out the wheat/grain) is the last emergency measure: after eating to restore strength, the crew dumps everything heavy to maximize buoyancy for the beaching attempt. The grain was the ship's commercial cargo and the centurion's military supply; its loss is total economic ruin for the voyage but the only way to float the ship close enough to shore to swim.
39: 'When daylight came, they did not recognise the land, but they saw a bay with a sandy beach, where they decided to run the ship aground if they could' — the bay (kolpon — bay, bosom) with a sandy beach (aigialon — shoreline, beach) is the landing site. St Paul's Bay in Malta is consistently identified as the location; it has a sandy beach and the right depth profile for the sounding sequence of Acts 27:28. 'They decided to run the ship aground' (ebouleusanto exōsai to ploion eis ton aigialon — they resolved to drive/push the ship onto the beach) — a controlled beaching is the only remaining option.
40: 'Cutting loose the anchors, they left them in the sea and at the same time untied the ropes that held the rudders. Then they hoisted the foresail to the wind and made for the beach' — the sequence of actions is precise seamanship: (1) cut all four stern anchors free; (2) release the lashings that had secured the twin steering-oars during the storm (so they could be used to steer); (3) hoist the artemon (foresail — the small forward sail used for maneuvering, not driving) to give steerage-way. The detail is the description of someone who watched skilled sailors execute a practiced emergency procedure.
41: 'But the ship struck a sandbar and ran aground. The bow stuck fast and would not move, and the stern was broken to pieces by the pounding of the surf' — 'a place where two seas met' (topon dithalassonkataschon — a place of two seas) identifies a sandbar or reef where two currents converge, creating a shallowing ridge. The ship's bow drives onto the bar and lodges; the waves continue to strike the exposed stern, breaking it apart. The destruction is exactly as Paul prophesied (Acts 27:22 — 'only the ship will be destroyed'): the ship breaks up while all 276 people survive.
42: 'The soldiers planned to kill the prisoners to prevent any of them from swimming away and escaping' — Roman military law held a guard personally responsible for the escape of a prisoner; the soldiers' proposal to kill the prisoners rather than risk escape is legally rational. If the prisoners escape, the soldiers face execution in their place. The proposal is murderous but comprehensible within the Roman accountability structure — the same logic drove the Philippian jailer to draw his sword (Acts 16:27).
43: 'But the centurion wanted to spare Paul's life and kept them from carrying out their plan. He ordered those who could swim to jump overboard first and get to land' — Julius's protection of Paul (boulomenos diasōsai ton Paulon — desiring to save Paul thoroughly) is the centurion's decisive act of loyalty to the one whose prophecy has sustained 276 people for two weeks. The ordered evacuation — swimmers first — is military discipline applied to salvation: the strongest go first to provide help for the weaker on shore.
44: 'The rest were to get there on planks or on other pieces of the ship. In this way everyone reached land safely' — 'everyone' (panta — all, every person) is the fulfillment-word: the divine promise of Acts 27:22 ('not one of you will be lost') and Acts 27:34 ('not a hair of any of you will be lost') is fulfilled precisely. 276 people entered the water and 276 people reached land. The 'pieces of the ship' (apo tōn apo tou ploiou — from the things from the ship) are the broken planks of the stern (Acts 27:41) — the ship's destruction becomes the instrument of the passengers' salvation: the broken ship saves them.
